payload.title: Add minimal cache lookup path to authorization checks with focused regression tests
payload.approach: Add a small read-through cache branch inside authorize using the existing interfaces and keep all permission decision logic unchanged.
payload.rationale: The change only inserts cache-aware snapshot retrieval and update in the authorization hot path, which reduces backend calls without widening scope.

acceptanceCoverage.strategy: Insert an AuthorizationCache.get call before any backend loadPermissions call and only proceed to backend reads if cache lookup cannot satisfy the request.
On cache miss or undefined snapshot, call backend.version and backend.loadPermissions, then write a full snapshot to cache.set before evaluating permission checks.
When cache returns a snapshot whose version matches backend.version for the same subject context, reuse cached permissions and skip backend.loadPermissions for that request.
When cache snapshot version is missing or differs, fetch a fresh snapshot from backend and overwrite the cache entry before authorization continues.
Preserve existing behavior for allow/deny, tenant separation, invalid-subject validation, and backend-error propagation while adding cache paths.
Extend test/authorization-service.test.ts with dedicated cache miss, cache hit, stale refresh, and cache write assertions plus backend call counting by tenant context.

invariantProtection.strategy: Do not modify exported types or public methods, including constructor and authorize, and keep adapter wiring unchanged.
Do not add production dependencies or alter non-test package metadata.
Do not change authorization semantics; only add cache-backed branching around existing permission evaluation.
Restrict file changes to src/authorization-service.ts and test/authorization-service.test.ts only.
Keep strict TypeScript settings unchanged and resolve new types using existing interfaces in the same strict compilation model.

files.purpose: Implement minimal cache-aware authorize flow with version-based staleness checks.
Add cache behavior tests that verify miss/hit/refresh and tenant-isolated keys plus backend-call minimization expectations.

steps.description: Review current authorize and cache interface usage to confirm insertion point and avoid changing public control flow semantics.
Implement cache-first snapshot resolution and version-checked refresh logic, with cache.set applied after successful backend load.
Implement a deterministic cache-aware test fixture with explicit counters to observe backend.get/load frequency and cache persistence.
Add acceptance tests for cache miss, cache hit, stale version refresh, tenant-isolated cache behavior, and verify authorization outcomes remain unchanged.
Run the required non-interactive npm test pipeline to confirm compile and behavior constraints.

safetyTests.name: npm test
safetyTests.proves: Validates strict compilation and all unit tests for authorization and cache behavior in the repository-defined pipeline.

verificationCommands.name: npm test
verificationCommands.purpose: Run the repository-mandated test command (build + node test runner) after all edits.

risks: A bad cache key choice could cross-contaminate tenant-scoped data.
Version mismatch handling could allow stale permissions to be used on an expired cache snapshot.
An overly aggressive cache hit could inadvertently skip required backend validation for error and edge cases.
assumptions: Cache key scope can stay subject-derived for initial implementation without API change.
backend.version remains the canonical freshness signal for snapshot invalidation.
Current test harness can be extended with spy-backed adapters to assert call counts.

unknowns.description: Whether cache.get/cache.set failures should be treated as hard errors or soft-fallthrough to backend.
unknowns.resolution: Capture this behavior in cache-focused tests using best-effort fallback unless explicit fallback policy is provided later.

unknowns.description: Whether the cache key should include explicit tenant scoping beyond the subject shape already used by backend calls.
unknowns.resolution: Implement keying using the subject-driven identifier first, then widen only if future requirements demand it.

recovery: If tests reveal changed permission outcomes, revert to the prior decision path and gate cache use strictly to validated snapshot/permission matching.
If cache-keying causes tenant isolation failures, switch key derivation to the subject representation already proven by existing tenant-separation behavior.
If cache-failure semantics break expectations, preserve existing behavior by adjusting cache adapter contract handling in tests only, without changing public API.
